title: Best Waterfalls in Costa Rica: 12 Spectacular Falls You Cannot Miss in 2026
excerpt: Costa Rica's topography makes it a waterfall-rich country, and the rainy season transforms minor streams into thundering cascades. From easily accessible falls surrounded by tourist infrastructure to remote cataracts reachable only by trail, this guide covers the waterfalls worth planning a trip around.
content:
Best Waterfalls in Costa Rica: 12 Spectacular Falls You Cannot Miss in 2026

Drive any mountain road in Costa Rica during the green season and you will quickly understand why the country has no shortage of waterfalls. The Talamanca and Central Volcanic mountain ranges intercept moisture-laden clouds off the Caribbean, and the resulting rainfall feeds a dense network of rivers that drop over volcanic ridges in spectacular fashion. Some of these falls are bucket-list sights; others reward the effort of reaching them with a solitude that commercial destinations rarely offer.

Nauyaca Waterfalls — The Iconic Southern Pacific Falls

The Nauyaca Waterfalls on the southern Pacific coast are arguably the most photographed waterfalls in Costa Rica, and for good reason. Two distinct falls — one approximately 20 meters across, the other dropping about 15 meters into a crystalline pool — create a scene that works equally well in wide compositions and intimate close-ups. A rough road climb leads to the falls, accessible by 4WD, horseback, or a demanding hike of about 5 kilometers each way.

The lower pool is deep enough for swimming and popular with locals on weekends. Photography works well throughout the day, though the falls face east and morning light produces the cleanest, least contrasty results. A drone shot from the ridge above gives a perspective that ground-level compositions simply cannot achieve.

La Fortuna Waterfall — The Northern Zone Showpiece

Just outside the town of La Fortuna, the Rio Fortuna drops 70 meters into a circular pool surrounded by dense forest. A steep trail of about 500 steps descends through farmland and then mature forest before arriving at the pool. The volume of water varies dramatically between the dry and wet seasons — peak flow in October delivers a thundering wall of water, while March and April see a noticeably thinner cascade. Either way, the sheer scale of the fall commands attention.

Swimming is permitted in the lower pool and the water is cold — genuinely cold, even on hot days. The rocky edge can be slippery, and the depth near the base of the falls is substantial. Many visitors spend an hour or more here, making this one of the most popular half-day excursions from the La Fortuna area.

Rio Celeste — The Blue River Falls

The Tenorio Volcano area is home to one of Costa Rica's most distinctive natural attractions: the Rio Celeste, where two tributaries converge and mineral deposits give the water an unmistakable turquoise-blue color. The main waterfall of the Rio Celeste is a moderate hike from the park entrance and drops through a gorge flanked by dense forest. The color is most vivid where the two rivers meet and where the water churns over rocks — still photographs struggle to capture the intensity of the color, which visitors consistently describe as more vivid in person.

El Avion Waterfall — A Secret Spot Near Manuel Antonio

Less visited than the main Manuel Antonio park but reachable from the same area, the El Avion waterfall sits on private land off the main Quepos road. A short trail leads to a 15-meter cascade set in a shady gorge. The surrounding forest attracts white-faced capuchins and a variety of bird species, making it a reasonable wildlife add-on to a waterfall visit in the area.

Tapantí National Park — The Overlooked Highland Falls

Tapantí, in the highland area between San José and the Orosí Valley, is one of Costa Rica's least-visited major parks despite containing some of the most impressive waterfalls in the country. The Rio Grande de Tárcoles runs through the park, fed by tributaries that cascade down the mountain slopes. The main overlooks along the trail system offer views of multiple falls, and the park's elevation — over 1,200 meters — keeps the forest cool and misty throughout the year.

Montezuma Falls — Accessible Waterfall Near the Beach Town

The small town of Montezuma, at the tip of the Nicoya Peninsula, has grown into a destination in its own right over the past two decades, and the Montezuma waterfall is one of its principal attractions. A short walk from town leads to a series of cascades with natural pools suitable for swimming. The lowest tier is the most accessible; the upper falls require a more demanding climb and reward the effort with fewer other visitors.

Poás Volcano Area — High-Altitude Falls

The Poás Volcano area sits at over 2,500 meters elevation, and the climate here is noticeably cooler and wetter than the Central Valley below. Small streams cascade down the volcanic slopes, and the high rainfall creates several impressive roadside falls accessible without a significant hike. The cloud forest surrounding the volcano produces atmospheric conditions — low cloud, wet vegetation, saturated colors — that work particularly well for photography.

Pura vida Tip: Shooting Waterfalls in Costa Rica

A neutral density filter is the single most useful piece of gear for waterfall photography in Costa Rica. The combination of forest canopy and often-overcast conditions still requires slower shutter speeds to produce that characteristic silky-water effect. A two to four stop ND filter handles most situations; a polarizer helps reduce glare on wet rocks and foliage. During the green season, bring rain protection for your gear and a lens cloth that stays in your pocket rather than your bag.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the best time of year to visit Costa Rica waterfalls?

The green season from May through November offers the highest water volume and most dramatic flow, especially from August through October. The dry season still has water in most falls, but the flow is noticeably reduced. For photography, the green season produces better flow; for trail conditions and access roads, the dry season is more reliable.

Are Costa Rica waterfalls safe for swimming?

Many of the major falls have established swimming areas in the lower pools, but conditions change with water volume. During high flow periods, currents near the base of large falls can be dangerous. Always check local conditions and heed any warnings posted at the site. Do not swim near the base of any powerful waterfall.

Which waterfalls are accessible without a long hike?

La Fortuna Waterfall requires a 500-step descent but no significant distance. The Nauyaca falls require either a rough vehicle, horseback, or a 5km hike each way. Tapantí National Park has overlooks accessible by car. Montezuma town waterfall is a short walk from town center.

Do I need a guide to find the best waterfalls?

For the major, established falls, signs and infrastructure make self-navigation straightforward. For Tapantí and more remote locations, a local guide helps avoid trail confusion and improves wildlife chances along the way. In areas with private land access, a local guide also handles permission arrangements.

Can I use a drone at Costa Rica waterfalls?

Commercial drone operation requires a permit from the civil aviation authority in Costa Rica. At national parks, drone use is generally prohibited. At private properties offering waterfall visits, the owner or operator sets the policy. When in doubt, ask before flying.
